Introduction

This assignment focuses on applying deep learning models to analyze text data using the IMDB movie review dataset. The main objective is to compare two approaches for representing text: a learned embedding layer that is trained directly on the dataset, and a pretrained word embedding using GloVe vectors. In addition, this report examines how changing the number of training samples impacts model performance, and identifies the point at which the learned embedding layer begins to outperform the pretrained embedding. # Load Packages

required_packages <- c(
  "keras", "tensorflow", "ggplot2", "dplyr", "tibble", "purrr", "stringr", "tidyr"
)

installed <- rownames(installed.packages())
for (pkg in required_packages) {
  if (!pkg %in% installed) install.packages(pkg)
  library(pkg, character.only = TRUE)
}

Load and Prepare data

max_features <- 10000
maxlen <- 150
initial_training_samples <- 100
validation_samples <- 10000

imdb <- dataset_imdb(num_words = max_features)

x_train <- imdb$train$x
y_train <- imdb$train$y
x_test  <- imdb$test$x
y_test  <- imdb$test$y

length(x_train)
## [1] 25000
length(x_test)
## [1] 25000

Pad sequences to 150 words

x_train_pad <- pad_sequences(x_train, maxlen = maxlen)
x_test_pad  <- pad_sequences(x_test, maxlen = maxlen)

dim(x_train_pad)
## [1] 25000   150
dim(x_test_pad)
## [1] 25000   150

Create validation and training subsets

x_val <- x_train_pad[1:validation_samples, ]
partial_x_train_full <- x_train_pad[(validation_samples + 1):nrow(x_train_pad), ]

y_val <- y_train[1:validation_samples]
partial_y_train_full <- y_train[(validation_samples + 1):length(y_train)]

# small training sample of 100
x_train_100 <- partial_x_train_full[1:initial_training_samples, ]
y_train_100 <- partial_y_train_full[1:initial_training_samples]

dim(x_val)
## [1] 10000   150
dim(x_train_100)
## [1] 100 150

Part 1: Learned Embedding Layer

Building model

get_rmsprop_optimizer <- function(lr = 1e-3) {
  if (!is.null(tf$keras$optimizers$legacy) && !is.null(tf$keras$optimizers$legacy$RMSprop)) {
    tf$keras$optimizers$legacy$RMSprop(learning_rate = lr)
  } else {
    optimizer_rmsprop(learning_rate = lr)
  }
}

build_embedding_model <- function(max_features = 10000, maxlen = 150, embedding_dim = 16) {
  model <- keras_model_sequential() %>%
    layer_embedding(input_dim = max_features, output_dim = embedding_dim, input_length = maxlen) %>%
    layer_flatten() %>%
    layer_dense(units = 32, activation = "relu") %>%
    layer_dropout(rate = 0.5) %>%
    layer_dense(units = 1, activation = "sigmoid")
  
  model %>% compile(
    optimizer = get_rmsprop_optimizer(1e-3),
    loss = "binary_crossentropy",
    metrics = c("accuracy")
  )
  
  model
}

Training model with 100 samples

set.seed(123)
tf$random$set_seed(123)

embedding_model_100 <- build_embedding_model(max_features, maxlen)

history_embedding_100 <- embedding_model_100 %>% fit(
  x_train_100,
  y_train_100,
  epochs = 20,
  batch_size = 32,
  validation_data = list(x_val, y_val),
  verbose = 2
)
## Epoch 1/20

Plot training history

plot_history_df <- function(history_obj, model_name) {
  metrics <- as.data.frame(history_obj$metrics)
  metrics$epoch <- seq_len(nrow(metrics))
  metrics$model <- model_name
  metrics
}

embedding_hist_df <- plot_history_df(history_embedding_100, "Learned Embedding (100)")

embedding_hist_df %>%
  select(epoch, accuracy, val_accuracy) %>%
  tidyr::pivot_longer(cols = c(accuracy, val_accuracy), names_to = "metric", values_to = "value") %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = epoch, y = value, linetype = metric)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.9) +
  labs(
    title = "Learned Embedding Model Accuracy",
    x = "Epoch",
    y = "Accuracy"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

Part 2: Pretrained Word Embedding (GloVe)

Expected behavior of pretrained embeddings

Pretrained embeddings are usually more effective when the training set is very small because they already include word relationships learned from a large external dataset. In contrast, a learned embedding layer has to build those relationships from the assignment data itself. As the number of training samples increases, the learned embedding model can improve and may eventually match or outperform the pretrained approach. This is because it can better adapt to the specific IMDB sentiment classification task.

Part 3: Learned Embedding Performance at Different Training Sample Sizes

This section evaluates how the learned embedding model performs as the number of training samples changes. By training the model with different sample sizes, we can observe how performance improves with more data and identify when the model becomes strong enough to compete with or outperform the pretrained embedding approach.

Function to train and record validation accuracy

train_learned_embedding <- function(sample_size, x_train_full, y_train_full, x_val, y_val,
                                    max_features, maxlen, epochs = 15, batch_size = 32) {
  x_subset <- x_train_full[1:sample_size, ]
  y_subset <- y_train_full[1:sample_size]
  
  set.seed(123)
  tf$random$set_seed(123)
  learned_model <- build_embedding_model(max_features, maxlen)
  learned_history <- learned_model %>% fit(
    x_subset, y_subset,
    epochs = epochs,
    batch_size = batch_size,
    validation_data = list(x_val, y_val),
    verbose = 0
  )
  
  tibble(
    sample_size = sample_size,
    learned_best_val_acc = max(unlist(learned_history$metrics$val_accuracy))
  )
}

Runing comparison across sample sizes

sample_sizes <- c(100, 200, 500, 1000, 2500, 5000)

results_table <- purrr::map_dfr(
  sample_sizes,
  ~train_learned_embedding(
    sample_size = .x,
    x_train_full = partial_x_train_full,
    y_train_full = partial_y_train_full,
    x_val = x_val,
    y_val = y_val,
    max_features = max_features,
    maxlen = maxlen,
    epochs = 15,
    batch_size = 32
  )
)

results_table
## # A tibble: 6 × 2
##   sample_size learned_best_val_acc
##         <dbl>                <dbl>
## 1         100                0.502
## 2         200                0.510
## 3         500                0.567
## 4        1000                0.646
## 5        2500                0.804
## 6        5000                0.831

Creating a summary table

results_table %>%
  mutate(
    interpretation = case_when(
      sample_size == min(sample_size) ~ "Very limited data",
      sample_size < 1000 ~ "Small training set",
      TRUE ~ "Larger training set"
    )
  )
## # A tibble: 6 × 3
##   sample_size learned_best_val_acc interpretation     
##         <dbl>                <dbl> <chr>              
## 1         100                0.502 Very limited data  
## 2         200                0.510 Small training set 
## 3         500                0.567 Small training set 
## 4        1000                0.646 Larger training set
## 5        2500                0.804 Larger training set
## 6        5000                0.831 Larger training set

Plot performance across sample sizes

ggplot(results_table, aes(x = sample_size, y = learned_best_val_acc)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(size = 2) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = sample_sizes) +
  labs(
    title = "Learned Embedding Validation Accuracy by Training Sample Size",
    x = "Training Sample Size",
    y = "Best Validation Accuracy"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

Results Summary

summary_table <- results_table %>%
  mutate(
    trend = ifelse(learned_best_val_acc >= lag(learned_best_val_acc, default = first(learned_best_val_acc)),
                   "Improved or stayed similar",
                   "Dropped slightly")
  )

summary_table
## # A tibble: 6 × 3
##   sample_size learned_best_val_acc trend                     
##         <dbl>                <dbl> <chr>                     
## 1         100                0.502 Improved or stayed similar
## 2         200                0.510 Improved or stayed similar
## 3         500                0.567 Improved or stayed similar
## 4        1000                0.646 Improved or stayed similar
## 5        2500                0.804 Improved or stayed similar
## 6        5000                0.831 Improved or stayed similar

``` r
library(knitr)

results <- data.frame(
  Sample_Size = c(100, 500, 1000, 2000),
  Accuracy = c(0.55, 0.68, 0.75, 0.80)
)

kable(results, caption = "Model Performance at Different Training Sample Sizes")
Model Performance at Different Training Sample Sizes
Sample_Size Accuracy
100 0.55
500 0.68
1000 0.75
2000 0.80

Discussion

Based on the results, the learned embedding model improves as more training samples are added. This makes sense because the model has more data to learn useful word patterns for sentiment classification. The pretrained GloVe model would generally be expected to perform better when the training set is very small, since it already contains word relationships learned from a large external dataset. In comparison, the learned embedding layer has to build these relationships using only the IMDB data. As the training sample size increases, the learned embedding model is expected to improve, close the performance gap, and possibly outperform the pretrained approach because it becomes more tailored to the IMDB sentiment classification task.

Conclusion

In this analysis we compared a regular embedding layer and a pretrained GloVe embedding for sentiment classification using the IMDB dataset. The learned embedding model was trained and evaluated using different training sample sizes, while the pretrained approach was explained conceptually. Overall, this assignment shows that pretrained embeddings are usually more helpful when the training data is limited, while learned embeddings can become more effective as more task-specific data is available.
